title: Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate
subtitle: The reported annual death rate from [infectious](#dod:communicable-diseases) and [parasitic diseases](#dod:parasitic-infection) per 100,000 people, based on the [underlying cause](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death) listed on death certificates.
caption:
Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate

The reported annual death rate from [infectious](#dod:communicable-diseases) and [parasitic diseases](#dod:parasitic-infection) per 100,000 people, based on the [underlying cause](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death) listed on death certificates.

This line chart tracks the age-standardized annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people across six countries from 1950 to 2023. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 400 deaths per 100,000, while the horizontal axis spans the years 1950 through 2023. South Africa displays a distinct trajectory compared to the other nations, with data beginning around 1997 at approximately 125 deaths per 100,000. The line for South Africa rises steeply to a peak near 390 around 2006, followed by a consistent decline to roughly 130 by 2023. In contrast, the lines for Brazil, Russia, the United States, France, Germany, and Australia remain clustered in the lower portion of the chart, generally staying below 75 deaths per 100,000 throughout the observed period. Brazil starts near 65 in 1980 and trends downward to about 30 by 2023. Russia shows a rise from roughly 25 in 1980 to a peak near 40 around 2000, before falling back to approximately 25. The United States, France, Germany, and Australia form a tight group at the bottom, with values consistently hovering between 10 and 20 deaths per 100,000 from 1950 to 2023. France begins near 40 in 1950 and drops to under 15 by 1970, maintaining that lower level thereafter. Germany and Australia follow similar low-level paths, starting near 40 in 1950 and settling into the 10-15 range by 1970. The United States line stays flat around 15-20 from 1980 to 2023. The data shows South Africa experiencing a much higher death rate than the other five countries during the late 1990s and 2000s, while all six nations show lower rates in 2023 compared to their respective peaks or starting points.
